taxonomy.label: Code Review & Quality
taxonomy.description: Reviewing diffs, finding bugs, refactoring, enforcing standards.

taxonomy.label: Security & Audits
taxonomy.description: Vulnerability assessment, threat modeling, pentest, trust boundaries.

taxonomy.label: Build & Prototyping
taxonomy.description: From idea to working app, UI generation, feature scaffolding.

taxonomy.label: Content & Writing
taxonomy.description: Blogs, marketing, humanizing text, CVs, docs.

taxonomy.label: Project Intelligence
taxonomy.description: Data extraction, dashboards, project radar, reporting.

taxonomy.label: Dev Workflow & Automation
taxonomy.description: Loops, browser automation, orchestration, environment setup.

taxonomy.label: Integrations & CRM
taxonomy.description: External APIs, CRM, content platforms, connectors.

taxonomy.label: Meta & Skill Tooling
taxonomy.description: Skills about skills: protocols, trust, tooling.

skills.name: cv-master
skills.tagline: Edits, translates, restyles or generates a CV/resume from a PDF, DOCX, link or raw data — outputs editable HTML + a verified PDF.
skills.verdict: Clean security surface: bundled scripts are read-only, and the one network call is a status-only ping with no data sent. Well-built overall. Weak spot: no version field or changelog, so you can't tell what changed between updates.

highlights.summary: Bundled scripts are read-only (poppler PDF triage, tool probes); the only network call is a status-code-only ping to fonts.googleapis.com with no data sent — verified safe.
Install hints (e.g. pip install pillow) are well-known default-registry packages but unpinned; user-run only.
No version field, changelog, or dated revision notes in SKILL.md or README.

skills.name: self-improving-agent
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (3.9k★): captures learnings, errors and corrections so the agent improves over time.
skills.verdict: Trustworthy on security — it writes learnings to its own workspace for its stated purpose, not exfiltration. Docs have a small internal slip (hyphenated format vs underscored examples) and the main file runs a bit long, but nothing that misleads you.

highlights.summary: Writes learnings to workspace files for its declared purpose — not secret exfiltration (a weak evaluator over-flags this).
The stated Pattern-Key format says hyphenated, but the doc's own examples use underscores — a self-inconsistency.
588-line SKILL.md is over the ~500-line budget (still under 2×).

skills.name: skill-vetter
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (1.3k★): a security-first checklist for vetting other skills before install. Meta — evaluated by a grader.
skills.verdict: Security-clean and its listed attack patterns are documentation, not danger. Its blind spot: it never says when NOT to use it, so it could misfire on plain code review instead of security vetting. Also assumes jq is installed without checking.

highlights.summary: Lists attack patterns (curl|bash, ~/.ssh, base64) as things to DETECT — correctly read as detection material, not an attack; layer-1 flags 3, the rubric de-escalates.
No 'NOT for' / out-of-scope guidance — could misfire on general code review.
jq is invoked with no requirements note or availability check.

skills.name: ontology
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (650★): a typed knowledge graph for structured agent memory and composable recall.
skills.verdict: Middling trust. It never states what it's out of scope for despite casting a wide net, so it can trigger on tasks it wasn't built for. It also lacks any version history, and it calls python3 and a script without checking they exist.

highlights.summary: No out-of-scope guidance despite broad triggers — high misfire risk.
No version field, changelog, or dated revision notes.
python3 and a bundled script are invoked with no requirements note or availability check.

skills.name: mcp-builder
skills.tagline: Official Anthropic skill for scaffolding MCP servers — protocol setup, tools, resources, and testing.
skills.verdict: Well-built: no attempt to steer or game an evaluator, instructions stay on-task. Only real gaps are hygiene — the mcp package dependency is pinned to a minimum version rather than exact, and there's no version field or changelog to track changes.

highlights.summary: The `mcp` package is pinned to a minimum version (>=1.1.0) rather than an exact version — minor supply-chain looseness.
No versioning signal (version field or changelog) in frontmatter or body.
No attempt to steer the evaluator; instructions serve the skill's task only.

skills.name: webapp-testing
skills.tagline: Official Anthropic skill for driving and testing web apps in a headless browser.
skills.verdict: Mixed bag. It never says when NOT to use it and never defines what the final output should look like or where it goes, leaving results ambiguous. On the plus side, its security footprint is clean with no network or destructive risk.

highlights.summary: The description and body never say when NOT to use the skill — no negative triggers.
Describes its capabilities but does not define a final deliverable format or location.
No version field, changelog, or dated revision notes.

skills.name: pdf
skills.tagline: Official Anthropic production skill for reading, filling, and manipulating PDF files.
skills.verdict: No scope boundaries at all: the description is broad with no 'don't use this for' guidance, so it can hijack unrelated tasks. It also never defines a deliverable format or output location for any of its operations, and its name doesn't even match its own directory.

highlights.summary: The description is very broad and does not specify when NOT to use the skill.
Describes many operations but defines no final deliverable format or location for any of them.
The name 'pdf' does not match the directory name 'pdf-processing' the skill ships under.

skills.name: skill-creator
skills.tagline: Official Anthropic skill for authoring new skills — scaffolding, structure, and evaluation guidance.
skills.verdict: Contradicts itself on a key rule — it forbids using /skill-test in one place, then tells you to skip evaluation elsewhere, so which instruction wins is unclear. Several other steps lean on vague judgment calls ('different metaphors') with no way to check if you did it right.

highlights.summary: Forbids using /skill-test in one place, then references skipping evaluation in the Claude.ai path — an internal contradiction.
Several instructions rely on subjective judgment ('different metaphors', 'something greater') without decidable criteria.
The __EVAL_DATA_PLACEHOLDER__ is a template placeholder, not a piped-shell command — correctly not flagged (weak models mis-flag this).

skills.name: humanizer
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (700★): strips AI-generated tells from text to give writing a more natural human voice.
skills.verdict: Clean text-only transformation — no network calls, no secret access, no code execution anywhere. Its only weakness is not saying when NOT to use it, so it could get invoked on writing tasks it wasn't meant for.

highlights.summary: Pure text transformation — no network, secret access, or code execution anywhere (security is n/a or pass throughout).
No 'NOT for' / out-of-scope guidance — could misfire on adjacent writing tasks.

skills.name: gog
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (941★): a CLI for Google Workspace — Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Contacts, Sheets and Docs.
skills.verdict: Handles your own OAuth token locally and only talks to Google's APIs — no secret exfiltration. But it has no failure handling: if the binary is missing, you're unauthenticated, or a token expired, nothing tells you what to do, and it installs from an unpinned personal Homebrew tap.

highlights.summary: Handles the user's own OAuth token locally and talks only to Google APIs — no secret exfiltration, harvesting, or unsolicited egress.
OAuth setup and every networked command can fail (missing binary, unauthenticated, expired token) with no failure handling.
Installs from an unpinned personal Homebrew tap (steipete/tap/gogcli).

skills.name: weather
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (423★): current weather and forecasts with no API key required.
skills.verdict: Solid trust: it fetches public weather data only, no secrets touched, nothing leaves your machine. Docs don't say when it shouldn't be used (e.g. historical climate data), and there's no version field to track changes.

highlights.summary: Fetches public weather data only — no secrets, no local data sent anywhere. Security clean.
No 'NOT for' / out-of-scope guidance (e.g. historical climate data).
No version field, changelog, or dated revision notes.

skills.name: imap-smtp-email
skills.tagline: ClawHub skill (107★): reads and sends mail over IMAP/SMTP — checks new mail, fetches and sends messages.
skills.verdict: Falls back to a plaintext password file in its own directory (600 permissions, app-passwords recommended) that never leaves your machine — a real but contained weakness. It also tells you to disable TLS certificate verification for self-signed certs, and its own docs disagree on which config file loads first.

highlights.summary: Falls back to a plaintext-password .env in the skill dir (600-perm, app-passwords recommended) — credentials stay local and are never echoed or sent anywhere but the user's own servers.
Advises *_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=false for self-signed certs — weakens TLS verification.
Config-precedence contradiction between lines 36 and 63 (which .env is checked first).

skills.name: superdesign
skills.tagline: ClawHub skill (192★): expert front-end guidelines for building modern, polished user interfaces.
skills.verdict: Its own frontmatter name doesn't match its directory and collides with another skill's name, which is confusing to route correctly. It also gives no guidance on when not to use it despite claiming to handle 'any user interface', and it loads an unpinned CDN script.

highlights.summary: Frontmatter name is 'frontend-design' but the directory is 'superdesign/' — a name mismatch that also collides with a different skill.
No 'NOT for' guidance; 'any user interface' invites misfire.
Loads lucide@latest from unpkg (unpinned CDN dependency).

skills.name: self-improving
skills.tagline: ClawHub (1.3k★): Self-reflection + Self-criticism + Self-learning + Self-organizing memory. Agent evaluates its own work, catches mistakes,
skills.verdict: Contradicts its own scope section: it lists an exhaustive 'ONLY does these four things' list, then does something else entirely elsewhere in the file. The name field also isn't proper kebab-case, and there's no guidance on when not to use it despite overlapping with several similar skills.

highlights.summary: name field is not kebab-case (spaces, uppercase, plus sign); a separate slug field is kebab-case but the check judges name.
No 'NOT for' / exclusion / pointer-to-other-skill language appears anywhere in the file, despite closely related skills (memory, learning, decide, escalate) being listed, so misfir
Contradicts the 'Scope' section's exhaustive 'This skill ONLY' list (lines 215-219), which enumerates four behaviors — none of which is writing to workspace AGENTS.md/SOUL.md/HEART

skills.name: proactive-agent
skills.tagline: ClawHub (821★): Transform AI agents from task-followers into proactive partners that anticipate needs and continuously improve. Now with WA
skills.verdict: Relies throughout on tools that may not exist (memory_search, cron, trash) with no check for whether they're available, so failures surface late. The description gives no 'use when' cue and no 'don't use for' boundary, so a dispatcher has little to go on for a broad, persistent behavior.

highlights.summary: Skill relies throughout on non-universal tools (memory_search, session_status, cron, trash) with no requirements/availability-check section anywhere; failures are only discovered a
Description states what the skill is/does and lists feature names, but gives no 'use when' cue, task type, or example phrasing a dispatching agent could match against.
No 'NOT for' / 'out of scope' / pointer-to-other-skill language appears anywhere in the description or body, despite this being a broad, persistent behavioral overlay that could ea

skills.name: multi-search-engine
skills.tagline: ClawHub (752★): Multi search engine integration with 16 engines (7 CN + 9 Global). Supports advanced search operators, time filters, site s
skills.verdict: Directly contradicts its own workflow: its core process fetches 16 external search URLs, which conflicts with what the skill claims elsewhere. It also never says when not to use it despite being a broad general-purpose search tool, and never defines what its final report should contain.

highlights.summary: Neither the description nor the body contains any 'NOT for' / out-of-scope / pointer-to-other-skill language, despite this being a broad general-purpose search skill likely to over
Directly contradicts the skill's own core workflow, which fetches 16 external search-engine URLs via web_fetch, e.g. line 16: 'Use web_fetch to execute search requests with rate li
No format, location, or required sections are specified for the 'core search report' deliverable.

skills.name: github
skills.tagline: ClawHub (652★): Interact with GitHub using the `gh` CLI. Use `gh issue`, `gh pr`, `gh run`, and `gh api` for issues, PRs, CI runs, and adva
skills.verdict: No scope boundaries despite covering all of general GitHub interaction, so it's prone to firing on unrelated tasks. It also has zero failure handling — if gh is missing, unauthenticated, or a call fails, nothing in the skill says what to do — and no version tracking at all.

highlights.summary: No version field in frontmatter, no changelog section, and no dated revision notes anywhere in the file.
No 'NOT for' / out-of-scope / pointer-to-other-skill language anywhere in the description or body; the skill's scope is broad enough (general GitHub interaction) that exclusions wo
No mention anywhere of what to do if `gh` is missing, unauthenticated, or a command/API call fails — total silence across a multi-step external-tool-dependent flow.

skills.name: free-ride
skills.tagline: ClawHub (474★): Manages free AI models from OpenRouter for OpenClaw. Automatically ranks models by quality, configures fallbacks for rate-l
skills.verdict: Its frontmatter name doesn't even match its own directory, a basic mismatch. It also gives no guidance on when not to use it despite broad triggers like 'reduce AI costs' that could catch unrelated cost-saving requests, and carries no version history. Quality itself scores well otherwise.

highlights.summary: Skill directory is free-ride but frontmatter name is freeride (no hyphen) — mismatch per H02 rule.
No version field in frontmatter, no changelog section, and no dated revision notes anywhere in the file.
No 'NOT for' / exclusion language anywhere in the file, despite broad triggers ('model switching', 'reduce AI costs') that could misfire on non-OpenRouter or non-OpenClaw cost/mode

skills.name: agent-browser-clawdbot
skills.tagline: ClawHub (446★): Headless browser automation CLI optimized for AI agents with accessibility tree snapshots and ref-based element selection
skills.verdict: Solid trust profile: security A, quality B. But the multi-step workflow -- network navigation, an external CLI, file-based state -- has zero guidance on failure: a broken command or missing file leaves you guessing. No version history, and no clear trigger phrasing in the description either.

highlights.summary: No version field in frontmatter, no changelog section, and no dated revision notes anywhere in the file.
The whole multi-step workflow (network navigation, external CLI tool, file-based state load/save, installation) has zero mention anywhere of what to do if a command fails, the tool
States what the tool is but names no concrete trigger situations or example user phrasings in the description field itself; the 'when to use' guidance only appears in the body (##

skills.name: obsidian
skills.tagline: ClawHub (444★): Work with Obsidian vaults (plain Markdown notes) and automate via obsidian-cli.
skills.verdict: Weak spot: no scope boundaries at all, despite covering search, create, move, and delete -- an agent could misfire on the wrong operation. Only the create path even discusses failure (URI handler issues); search, move, and delete are left silent if something goes wrong. Security is clean though.

highlights.summary: No version field in frontmatter, no changelog section, and no dated revision notes anywhere in the file.
No 'NOT for' / exclusion / out-of-scope guidance appears anywhere in the description or body, despite the skill covering multiple distinct operations (search, create, move, delete)
Only the create path gets any failure mention (URI handler must be working, may be refused for dot-folders); search, move, delete, and 'obsidian-cli not installed' are left with ze

skills.name: auto-updater
skills.tagline: ClawHub (441★): Automatically update Clawdbot and all installed skills once daily. Runs via cron, checks for updates, applies them, and mes
skills.verdict: Concrete risk: setup adds a cron job by name with no check for an existing one first -- re-run the Quick Start and you get duplicate scheduled updates running side by side. No stated exclusion cases either. Security and hygiene are otherwise clean (both A).

highlights.summary: No 'NOT for' / exclusion / out-of-scope guidance anywhere in description or body; skill has multiple sub-flows (setup, manual commands, troubleshooting) so misfire is not implausib
Setup instructs adding a cron job by name with no check for an existing 'Daily Auto-Update' job first; re-running the Quick Start/manual setup would create duplicate cron entries.
Describes what the skill does in detail but never states a triggering phrase or 'use when' cue for a dispatching agent.

skills.name: nano-banana-pro
skills.tagline: ClawHub (415★): Generate/edit images with Nano Banana Pro (Gemini 3 Pro Image). Use for image create/modify requests incl. edits. Supports
skills.verdict: Strong showing: quality A, security A -- the core capability looks well built and safe. Remaining gaps are bookkeeping: no version or changelog to track changes, and no stated 'not for' cases to stop it being reached for adjacent image tasks it doesn't cover.

highlights.summary: No version field, changelog, or dated revision notes anywhere in the file.
No 'NOT for' / exclusion / pointer-to-other-skill language anywhere in description or body.

skills.name: api-gateway
skills.tagline: ClawHub (392★): Connect to external services through Maton-managed API routes. Use this skill only after the user names the target app, acc
skills.verdict: Main weakness is bloat: roughly 1555 lines, three times the normal budget, including a near-duplicate ~150-line provider list right after the main services table. With ~150 services covered and no exclusion guidance, misfiring against a more specific skill is a real risk. Security stays clean.

highlights.summary: File is 1555 lines / ~69KB, roughly 3x the ~500-line/25KB budget (bulk Supported Services table + duplicated per-provider reference link list inline).
No 'NOT for'/out-of-scope/pointer-to-other-skill language appears anywhere in the description or body, despite the skill covering ~150 different services where misfire against dedi
Beyond the 150+ row Supported Services table, a second ~150-line list duplicating the same providers with links follows this line, all inline in SKILL.md despite an obvious per-pro

skills.name: desktop-control
skills.tagline: ClawHub (377★): Advanced desktop automation with mouse, keyboard, and screen control
skills.verdict: Serious defect: the frontmatter has no name field at all, just a description. Without a name, the skill may fail to register or load correctly, breaking things before it even runs. No versioning either. This needs fixing before use, not a minor nitpick.

highlights.summary: Frontmatter block (lines 1-3) contains only a `description` field; there is no `name` field at all, so the required non-empty name is absent.
No `name` value exists in frontmatter to check against kebab-case or the directory name (`desktop-control/`) — naming requirement is violated by omission.
No version field in frontmatter, no changelog heading, and no dated revision notes anywhere in the document.

skills.name: word-docx
skills.tagline: ClawHub (352★): Create, inspect, and edit Microsoft Word documents and DOCX files with reliable styles, numbering, tracked changes, tables,
skills.verdict: Red flag: this skill is pure prose -- no named commands, tools, or scripts anywhere, just conceptual instructions like 'inspect the package layout.' An agent following it has nothing concrete to execute and may improvise wrongly. Its name field also breaks kebab-case, and no scope boundary is stated.

highlights.summary: name is not kebab-case (spaces, slash, uppercase) against ^[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$; the separate `slug: word-docx` field matches the directory but the rubric judges the `name` fiel
The 'Related Skills' list names adjacent skills to install alongside, but nowhere does the file say 'NOT for X, use Y instead' or otherwise state out-of-scope cases; no exclusion p

skills.name: youtube-watcher
skills.tagline: ClawHub (336★): Fetch and read transcripts from YouTube videos. Use when you need to summarize a video, answer questions about its content,
skills.verdict: Main gap: no defined output -- the skill never says where the result goes or what shape it takes, so you may not know where to find what it produced. Failure handling only covers missing captions; a missing yt-dlp install, an equally likely failure, has no stated fallback. Security is clean.

highlights.summary: No 'NOT for'/exclusion language appears anywhere in the file; nothing distinguishes this from non-YouTube video/audio summarization requests.
No format, location, or shape is defined for either the script's output or the final deliverable — matches the rubric's own fail example almost verbatim.

skills.tagline: ClawHub (334★): Local speech-to-text with the Whisper CLI (no API key).
skills.verdict: Strong core: quality A, security A. The main gaps are housekeeping -- no version or changelog to track changes, and the Homebrew install isn't pinned to a version (though it's the standard registry with an exact, well-known formula name, so typosquat risk is low).

highlights.summary: No version field in frontmatter, no changelog section, and no dated revision notes anywhere in the file.
